GS2.headline: Supreme Court Reinforces Right to Privacy, Issues Guidelines on Digital Surveillance
GS2.explainer:
• The Supreme Court, in a landmark ruling, reiterated the inviolability of the Right to Privacy under Article 21, asserting that arbitrary digital surveillance by the state is unconstitutional.
• The judgment established a stringent three-part test for any state surveillance: legality, necessity, and proportionality, with judicial oversight as a mandatory component.
• It mandated the establishment of an independent oversight mechanism, possibly involving retired judges or technical experts, to review and approve surveillance requests.
• The court also directed the government to frame a comprehensive data protection law that specifically addresses state access to personal data and ensures accountability.
• This ruling is seen as a significant step towards balancing national security concerns with individual civil liberties in the digital age.
gsSubTopics: Fundamental Rights
Judiciary
Government policies & interventions
Information Technology
prelimsPoints: Right to Privacy is part of Article 21 (Right to Life and Personal Liberty).
K.S. Puttaswamy judgment (2017) declared privacy a fundamental right.
Three-part test for surveillance: legality, necessity, proportionality.
Mandatory judicial oversight for surveillance requests.
Directive to frame comprehensive data protection law.
mainsPoints: Discuss the evolving interpretation of the Right to Privacy in the digital era.
Analyze the challenges in balancing national security with individual civil liberties in surveillance policies.
Evaluate the role of the judiciary in upholding fundamental rights against state overreach.
Suggest reforms required in India's legal framework for digital surveillance and data protection.
Examine the international best practices in surveillance oversight and data governance.
GS2.imageDescription: A gavel resting on a law book with a blurred background of binary code, symbolizing legal decisions in the digital realm.
GS2.trivia: The 'reasonable expectation of privacy' test originated in the US Supreme Court case Katz v. United States (1967).

GS2.headline: India-EU Free Trade Agreement Nears Conclusion, Poised to Reshape Bilateral Trade
GS2.explainer:
• Negotiations for the ambitious India-European Union Free Trade Agreement (FTA) are reportedly in their final stages, with both sides expressing optimism for an imminent signing.
• The agreement aims to significantly reduce tariffs on a wide range of goods and services, potentially boosting bilateral trade by over 40% in the next five years.
• Key sectors expected to benefit include automobiles, textiles, pharmaceuticals, agricultural products, and digital services, with provisions for intellectual property rights and sustainable development.
• While sensitive issues like market access for specific agricultural products and data localization remain, negotiators are confident of finding common ground.
• This FTA is strategically important for India to diversify its trade partnerships and enhance its position in global supply chains, while offering the EU access to a vast and growing market.
gsSubTopics: Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests
India and its neighbourhood- relations
Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment
prelimsPoints: FTA aims to boost India-EU bilateral trade by over 40%.
Key sectors: automobiles, textiles, pharmaceuticals, agriculture, digital services.
EU is India's third-largest trading partner.
FTA includes provisions on IPR and sustainable development.
Negotiations started in 2007, resumed in 2021 after a hiatus.
mainsPoints: Analyze the economic implications of the India-EU FTA for various sectors in India.
Discuss the strategic significance of this FTA in the context of India's 'Act East' policy and global trade diversification.
Evaluate the challenges and opportunities for Indian businesses arising from increased competition and market access in the EU.
Examine how the FTA addresses modern trade concerns like environmental standards, labor rights, and digital trade.
Critique the role of FTAs in shaping global economic order and India's position within it.
GS2.imageDescription: Flags of India and the European Union superimposed over a graph showing upward economic trends.
GS2.trivia: The EU is a unique economic and political union of 27 member states that are located primarily in Europe.

GS2.headline: BRICS Summit 2025 Concludes with Focus on Multilateralism and Global South Development
GS2.explainer:
• The annual BRICS Summit, held in Cape Town, South Africa, concluded with a joint declaration emphasizing strengthening multilateralism and promoting the interests of the Global South.
• Member nations reiterated their commitment to reforming global governance institutions, including the UN Security Council, and ensuring greater representation for developing countries.
• Discussions focused on enhancing intra-BRICS trade and investment, exploring mechanisms for de-dollarization, and strengthening cooperation in areas like climate change, health, and digital transformation.
• New members, admitted in the previous summit, actively participated, contributing to discussions on expanding the bloc's influence and fostering inclusive growth.
• The summit underscored BRICS's growing role as a counterweight to traditional Western-dominated forums and a voice for emerging economies.
gsSubTopics: Important International institutions, agencies and fora—their structure, mandate
India’s interests in global groupings
Effect of policies and politics of developed and developing countries on India’s interests
prelimsPoints: BRICS member countries (original and expanded).
Host city of the 2025 summit: Cape Town, South Africa.
Key agenda points: multilateralism, Global South, de-dollarization.
New Development Bank (NDB) is a BRICS initiative.
BRICS aims to reform global governance institutions.
mainsPoints: Analyze the evolving role of BRICS in the contemporary multipolar world order.
Discuss India's strategic interests and contributions within the BRICS framework.
Evaluate the feasibility and implications of de-dollarization efforts by BRICS nations.
Examine the challenges and opportunities presented by BRICS expansion for its cohesion and effectiveness.
Critique the potential impact of BRICS on global economic and political power dynamics.
GS2.imageDescription: Leaders of BRICS nations shaking hands at the summit venue in Cape Town, South Africa.
GS2.trivia: The acronym BRIC was coined in 2001 by Goldman Sachs economist Jim O'Neill to describe the four emerging economies of Brazil, Russia, India, and China.

GS2.headline: Indian Navy Concludes Major Multilateral Exercise in Indo-Pacific, Enhancing Maritime Security
GS2.explainer:
• The Indian Navy successfully concluded a large-scale multilateral naval exercise, 'Malabar Plus', in the Indo-Pacific region, involving navies from key partner nations.
• The exercise focused on interoperability, anti-submarine warfare, maritime surveillance, and humanitarian assistance/disaster relief (HADR) operations.
• Participating nations included members of the Quad, alongside other regional partners, underscoring a collective commitment to a free, open, and inclusive Indo-Pacific.
• The drills aimed at enhancing maritime domain awareness and strengthening cooperation amidst evolving geopolitical dynamics and increasing challenges to rules-based international order.
• This exercise reaffirms India's growing role as a net security provider and a crucial partner in maintaining stability in the strategically vital Indo-Pacific.
gsSubTopics: India and its neighbourhood- relations
Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests
Security challenges and their management in border areas
prelimsPoints: Exercise 'Malabar Plus' conducted in Indo-Pacific.
Involved Quad members and other regional partners.
Focus on anti-submarine warfare, maritime surveillance, HADR.
Aims to enhance interoperability and regional stability.
India is a key player in the Indo-Pacific strategy.
mainsPoints: Discuss the strategic importance of the Indo-Pacific region for India and its partners.
Analyze how multilateral naval exercises like 'Malabar Plus' contribute to regional security and stability.
Evaluate the role of India as a net security provider in the Indo-Pacific.
Examine the challenges posed by geopolitical rivalries and maritime disputes in the region.
Critique the effectiveness of such drills in deterring aggressive actions and upholding international law.
GS2.imageDescription: Warships from multiple navies sailing in formation during a joint exercise on the open sea.
GS2.trivia: The original Malabar exercise started in 1992 as a bilateral exercise between the Indian and US navies.

GS2.headline: Lokpal and Lokayukta Act: Annual Report Highlights Progress and Challenges in Anti-Corruption Efforts
GS2.explainer:
• The Lokpal of India presented its annual report, detailing the progress made in investigating corruption complaints against public functionaries and the challenges encountered.
• The report highlighted an increase in the number of cases processed, demonstrating the institution's growing functional capacity and outreach.
• However, it also pointed out delays in the appointment of Lokayuktas in several states and issues related to the timely submission of property declarations by public servants.
• Recommendations included strengthening the investigative wing of the Lokpal, ensuring financial and administrative autonomy, and greater public awareness campaigns about its mandate.
• The report underscores the ongoing efforts to enhance transparency and accountability in governance, while also emphasizing the need for robust institutional support.
gsSubTopics: Statutory, regulatory and various quasi-judicial bodies
Governance, transparency and accountability
Role of civil services in a democracy
prelimsPoints: Lokpal is an anti-corruption ombudsman for the Union government.
Lokayukta is the state-level equivalent.
Lokpal and Lokayukta Act was passed in 2013.
Jurisdiction includes Prime Minister (with caveats), Ministers, MPs, Group A, B, C, D officers.
Challenges include appointments and property declarations.
mainsPoints: Discuss the objectives and evolution of the Lokpal and Lokayukta institutions in India.
Analyze the achievements and inherent challenges in the functioning of the Lokpal and Lokayuktas.
Evaluate the effectiveness of these bodies in combating corruption and promoting good governance.
Examine the role of institutional independence and adequate resources for anti-corruption watchdogs.
Critique the legislative and administrative reforms needed to strengthen the Lokpal and Lokayukta framework.
GS2.imageDescription: A balance scale with 'Transparency' on one side and 'Corruption' on the other, with a gavel nearby.
GS2.trivia: The concept of an ombudsman originated in Sweden in 1809.

GS2.headline: India Advocates for Equitable Access to Vaccines and Medicines at Global Health Summit
GS2.explainer:
• India's delegation at the Global Health Summit stressed the urgent need for a new international treaty on pandemic preparedness and equitable access to essential health products.
• The summit, attended by health ministers and experts worldwide, focused on strengthening global health architecture and addressing disparities revealed by recent health crises.
• India highlighted its role as a 'pharmacy of the world' and called for technology transfer mechanisms to boost local manufacturing capacities in developing nations.
• Discussions also covered intellectual property waivers for crucial medical technologies during health emergencies and collaborative research efforts.
• The initiative reflects India's commitment to global health solidarity and its leadership in advocating for the Global South's health interests.
gsSubTopics: Important International institutions, agencies and fora—their structure, mandate
Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Health, Education, Human Resources
prelimsPoints: India advocates for a new pandemic preparedness treaty.
Emphasizes equitable access to vaccines and medicines.
India known as 'pharmacy of the world'.
Discussions on IP waivers and technology transfer.
WHO is a key international health organization.
mainsPoints: Discuss India's leadership role in global health diplomacy and its vision for a more equitable health architecture.
Analyze the challenges in ensuring universal and equitable access to essential health products during pandemics.
Evaluate the arguments for and against intellectual property waivers for medical technologies in health emergencies.
Examine the importance of technology transfer and local manufacturing in strengthening global health resilience.
Critique the current global health governance framework and suggest reforms for better pandemic preparedness.
GS2.imageDescription: A globe surrounded by medical symbols and diverse hands, symbolizing global health cooperation.
GS2.trivia: The World Health Organization (WHO) was established on 7 April 1948, celebrated as World Health Day.

GS3.headline: India Unveils 'Green Hydrogen for Bharat' Phase II: Boosting Domestic Production and Exports
GS3.explainer:
• The Union Cabinet approved Phase II of the National Green Hydrogen Mission, branded 'Green Hydrogen for Bharat', with enhanced incentives for production and infrastructure development.
• The new phase focuses on scaling up green hydrogen production capacity to 10 million metric tonnes per annum (MMTPA) by 2030 and attracting over $100 billion in investments.
• Key components include dedicated manufacturing zones, R&D funding for advanced electrolyser technologies, and export promotion strategies for green hydrogen and its derivatives.
• The mission aims to significantly reduce India's reliance on fossil fuel imports, create 600,000 jobs, and contribute to achieving net-zero emissions by 2070.
• Subsidies and viability gap funding will be provided for early adopters in hard-to-abate sectors like steel, cement, and heavy-duty transport.
gsSubTopics: Energy
Infrastructure
Environmental pollution & degradation
Government policies & interventions
Investment models
prelimsPoints: Phase II target: 10 MMTPA green hydrogen production by 2030.
Expected investment: Over $100 billion.
Job creation target: 600,000.
Nodal Ministry: Ministry of New and Renewable Energy.
Key components: SIGHT (Strategic Interventions for Green Hydrogen Transition) Programme, R&D initiatives.
mainsPoints: Discuss India's energy security and geopolitical implications of green hydrogen leadership.
Analyze the economic potential and challenges in scaling up green hydrogen production and export.
Evaluate the role of government incentives and private sector participation in achieving mission targets.
Examine the environmental benefits and contribution to India's climate commitments.
Critique the technological hurdles and infrastructure requirements for widespread adoption.
GS3.imageDescription: Illustration of a green hydrogen production plant with solar panels and wind turbines in the background.
GS3.trivia: Hydrogen is the most abundant chemical substance in the universe, making up roughly 75% of all normal matter.

GS3.headline: ISRO Successfully Conducts Critical Test Flight for Reusable Launch Vehicle (RLV)
GS3.explainer:
• The Indian Space Research Organisation (ISRO) achieved a significant milestone by successfully conducting a crucial test flight of its Reusable Launch Vehicle (RLV) Technology Demonstrator.
• The sub-orbital flight involved a controlled descent and autonomous landing, demonstrating key technologies for future low-cost access to space.
• This test is a step towards developing a two-stage-to-orbit (TSTO) fully reusable launch vehicle, which aims to drastically reduce the cost of space launches.
• The RLV program is crucial for India's ambitions in space commercialization, satellite deployment, and future human spaceflight missions (Gaganyaan).
• Further tests are planned to validate aerodynamic characteristics, thermal protection systems, and autonomous navigation for a full-scale reusable orbital vehicle.
gsSubTopics: Achievements of Indians in science & technology; indigenization of technology and developing new technology
Space technology
prelimsPoints: ISRO's RLV-TD program aims for low-cost space access.
The test involved controlled descent and autonomous landing.
RLV is a step towards a two-stage-to-orbit (TSTO) vehicle.
Crucial for future space commercialization and Gaganyaan mission.
India is among few nations developing RLV technology.
mainsPoints: Discuss the significance of reusable launch vehicle technology for India's space program and its global competitiveness.
Analyze the economic benefits of reducing launch costs for satellite deployment and space exploration.
Evaluate the technological challenges involved in developing fully reusable launch systems.
Examine the role of ISRO in fostering indigenous technological capabilities and promoting 'Atmanirbhar Bharat' in space.
Critique the strategic implications of advanced space capabilities for national security and international relations.
GS3.imageDescription: An artistic rendering of ISRO's Reusable Launch Vehicle (RLV) during re-entry into Earth's atmosphere.
GS3.trivia: The first successful orbital reusable spacecraft was the Space Shuttle, developed by NASA, which had its maiden flight in 1981.

GS3.headline: New National Cyber Security Framework 2.0 Unveiled to Safeguard Critical Infrastructure
GS3.explainer:
• The Ministry of Electronics and Information Technology (MeitY) launched the National Cyber Security Framework 2.0, focusing on enhanced protection for critical information infrastructure.
• The updated framework emphasizes a multi-stakeholder approach, promoting greater collaboration between government agencies, private sector entities, and academic institutions.
• Key features include a robust incident response mechanism, mandatory security audits for critical sectors, and a national-level cyber threat intelligence sharing platform.
• It also introduces stricter compliance norms for data centers and cloud service providers, along with initiatives for skill development in cybersecurity.
• The framework aims to bolster India's resilience against sophisticated cyber threats and ensure the security of its digital economy.
gsSubTopics: Cyber security
Internal security challenges
Information Technology

Critique the balance between state surveillance for security and safeguarding individual data privacy in the digital age.
GS3.imageDescription: A digital padlock icon superimposed on a map of India, representing national cybersecurity.
GS3.trivia: The first National Cyber Security Policy of India was released in 2013.

GS3.headline: Government Expands PLI Scheme to Boost Advanced Chemistry Cell (ACC) Battery Manufacturing
GS3.explainer:
• The Union Government announced an expansion of the Production Linked Incentive (PLI) scheme, specifically targeting Advanced Chemistry Cell (ACC) battery manufacturing in India.
• This move aims to establish a robust domestic supply chain for electric vehicles (EVs) and renewable energy storage, reducing reliance on imports.
• The expanded scheme offers enhanced incentives for companies investing in Giga-factories for ACC battery production, covering a wider range of battery technologies.
• It is expected to attract significant foreign direct investment (FDI) and foster indigenous research and development in battery technology.
• The initiative aligns with India's climate goals and 'Make in India' vision, positioning the country as a global hub for green technology manufacturing.
gsSubTopics: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment
Infrastructure: Energy
Investment models
Science and Technology - developments and their applications

GS3.imageDescription: A graphic showing various types of battery cells with the 'Make in India' logo.
GS3.trivia: The global demand for lithium-ion batteries is projected to grow significantly, driven primarily by the electric vehicle market.

GS3.headline: Indian Scientists Achieve Breakthrough in Gene Editing for Inherited Rare Blood Disorders
GS3.explainer:
• A team of Indian scientists at the National Institute of Biomedical Genomics (NIBMG) announced a significant breakthrough in gene editing technology for treating inherited rare blood disorders.
• The research focused on developing novel CRISPR-Cas9 based therapies to correct genetic mutations responsible for diseases like Thalassemia and Sickle Cell Anemia.
• The preclinical studies showed promising results in correcting disease-causing genes in patient-derived cells, paving the way for potential clinical trials.
• This advancement holds immense promise for millions suffering from rare genetic disorders, offering a curative approach rather than symptomatic treatment.
• Ethical considerations surrounding gene editing and the need for robust regulatory frameworks were also highlighted by the research team.
gsSubTopics: Biotechnology and issues relating to Intellectual Property Rights
Health
Science and Technology - developments and their applications

GS3.imageDescription: A stylized DNA helix with glowing segments, representing gene editing at a molecular level.
GS3.trivia: The Nobel Prize in Chemistry 2020 was awarded to Emmanuelle Charpentier and Jennifer A. Doudna for the development of a method for genome editing.

GS3.headline: Indian Scientists Discover New Species of Amphibian in Eastern Himalayas, Highlighting Biodiversity Richness
GS3.explainer:
• A team of Indian herpetologists announced the discovery of a new species of frog in the remote biodiversity hotspots of the Eastern Himalayas.
• The discovery underscores the immense, yet underexplored, biodiversity of the Himalayan region and its ecological fragility.
• Genetic analysis confirmed its distinctiveness, highlighting the unique evolutionary pressures in the region's diverse microhabitats.
• Researchers emphasized the urgent need for enhanced conservation efforts in the Eastern Himalayas, which faces threats from climate change, habitat loss, and unregulated tourism.
• This finding contributes significantly to India's taxonomic knowledge and reinforces the importance of protecting pristine ecosystems.
gsSubTopics: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment
Biodiversity
prelimsPoints: New species of frog discovered in Eastern Himalayas.
Eastern Himalayas is a biodiversity hotspot.
Discovery highlights ecological fragility and need for conservation.
Genetic analysis used for confirmation.
Amphibians are bio-indicators of environmental health.
mainsPoints: Discuss the significance of new species discoveries for understanding biodiversity and ecosystem health.
Analyze the unique ecological characteristics of the Eastern Himalayas and the threats it faces.
Evaluate the role of scientific research in informing conservation policies and practices.
Examine the challenges in exploring and protecting biodiversity in remote and inaccessible regions.
Critique the impact of human activities on fragile ecosystems and the importance of sustainable development.
GS3.imageDescription: A close-up photo of a newly discovered frog species camouflaged in moss and leaves.
GS3.trivia: The Eastern Himalayas are home to over 10,000 species of plants, 300 species of mammals, 977 species of birds, and 176 species of reptiles and amphibians.

GS3.headline: RBI Report Highlights Surge in Digital Payments in Rural India, Driving Financial Inclusion
GS3.explainer:
• A report by the Reserve Bank of India (RBI) indicates a significant surge in digital payment adoption across rural and semi-urban areas, fueled by government initiatives.
• The growth is attributed to the expansion of the Unified Payments Interface (UPI) network, Jan Dhan accounts, and increasing mobile penetration.
• This trend is contributing significantly to financial inclusion, enabling easier access to financial services for previously unbanked populations.
• Challenges remain in terms of digital literacy, infrastructure reliability in remote areas, and addressing cybersecurity concerns among rural users.
• The RBI emphasized continued efforts to enhance digital payment infrastructure, promote financial education, and ensure robust security measures to sustain this growth.
gsSubTopics: Inclusive growth and issues arising from it
Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment
Government policies & interventions

GS3.imageDescription: A rural Indian woman using a smartphone to make a digital payment at a local shop.
GS3.trivia: India's UPI system processed over 10 billion transactions in a single month for the first time in August 2023.
